audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua Ai ambush, king execution, Ebal altar, and public Torah-reading narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for renewed courage, plunder permission, ambush instructions, thirty-thousand/five-thousand figures, destruction of Ai, king hanging and burial, Mount Ebal altar, Torah inscription, Gerizim/Ebal assembly, and inclusive public reading.

verses.restored: YHWH said to Joshua, Do not fear and do not be shattered. Take with you all people of war, rise and go up to Ai. See, I have given into your hand king of Ai, his people, his city, and his land.
You shall do to Ai and its king as you did to Jericho and its king; only its spoil and livestock you may plunder for yourselves. Set for yourself an ambush for city from behind it.
Joshua rose and all people of war to go up to Ai; Joshua chose thirty thousand men, mighty ones of strength, and sent them by night.
He commanded them, saying, See, you shall lie in ambush for city from behind city. Do not be very far from city, and all of you be ready.
I and all people who are with me will draw near to city. It shall be, when they come out to meet us as at first, that we will flee before them.
They will come out after us until we draw them away from city, for they will say, They are fleeing before us as at first; and we will flee before them.
Then you shall rise from ambush and possess city, and YHWH your Divine will give it into your hand.
It shall be, when you capture city, you shall set city on fire; according to word of YHWH you shall do. See, I have commanded you.
Joshua sent them, and they went to place of ambush and dwelt between Bethel and Ai, west of Ai; and Joshua lodged that night in midst of people.
Joshua rose early in morning and mustered people, and he and elders of Israel went up before people to Ai.
All people of war who were with him went up and drew near and came before city and camped north of Ai, and valley was between him and Ai.
He took about five thousand men and set them as ambush between Bethel and Ai, west of city.
They set people, all camp that was north of city and its rear guard west of city; and Joshua went that night into midst of valley.

It was, when king of Ai saw, that men of city hurried and rose early and went out to meet Israel for battle, he and all his people, at appointed place before Arabah; but he did not know there was an ambush against him behind city.
Joshua and all Israel let themselves be struck before them and fled by way of wilderness.
All people who were in city were called to pursue after them; they pursued Joshua and were drawn away from city.
Not a man remained in Ai or Bethel who did not go out after Israel; they left city open and pursued Israel.
YHWH said to Joshua, Stretch out javelin that is in your hand toward Ai, for into your hand I will give it. Joshua stretched out javelin that was in his hand toward city.
The ambush rose quickly from its place and ran when he stretched out his hand; they came to city and captured it and hurried and set city on fire.

Men of Ai turned behind them and saw, and look, smoke of city went up to heavens; there was no hand/place for them to flee this way or that, and people fleeing toward wilderness turned against pursuers.
Joshua and all Israel saw that ambush had captured city and smoke of city went up, and they turned and struck men of Ai.
The others came out from city to meet them, and they were in midst of Israel, some from this side and some from that side; they struck them until no survivor or fugitive remained.
King of Ai they captured alive and brought him near to Joshua.

It was, when Israel finished killing all inhabitants of Ai in field, in wilderness where they pursued them, and all of them had fallen by edge of sword until they came to an end, that all Israel returned to Ai and struck it by edge of sword.
All who fell that day, from man to woman, were twelve thousand, all people of Ai.
Joshua did not draw back his hand that he stretched out with javelin until he devoted to destruction all inhabitants of Ai.
Only livestock and spoil of that city Israel plundered for themselves, according to word of YHWH that he commanded Joshua.
Joshua burned Ai and made it a heap forever, a desolation to this day.

King of Ai he hanged upon tree until time of evening; at going down of sun Joshua commanded, and they took his corpse down from tree and threw it at entrance of gate of city and raised over it a great heap of stones, to this day.
Then Joshua built an altar to YHWH Divine of Israel on Mount Ebal,

as Moses servant of YHWH commanded children of Israel, as written in scroll of Torah of Moses: an altar of whole/uncut stones over which no one wielded iron. They offered upon it burnt offerings to YHWH and sacrificed peace/well-being offerings.
There he wrote upon stones a copy of Torah of Moses, which he wrote before children of Israel.

All Israel, its elders, officers, and judges stood on this side and that side of ark before Levitical priests carrying ark of covenant of YHWH—the sojourner as native-born—half toward Mount Gerizim and half toward Mount Ebal, as Moses servant of YHWH commanded at first, to bless people of Israel.
Afterward he read all words of Torah, blessing and curse, according to all written in scroll of Torah.
There was not a word from all Moses commanded that Joshua did not read before all assembly of Israel, with women, little ones, and sojourners walking in their midst.

translation_notes: The chapter repeats Jericho's total-war pattern while permitting Israel to keep livestock and spoil, a change that follows the Achan crisis but does not make the conquest ethical.
Verses 3 and 12 give thirty-thousand and five-thousand ambush forces. These may describe separate detachments, compositional layers, or textual difficulties and require witness comparison.
Verse 17 includes Bethel in the Masoretic text, though narrative and textual questions remain about its role in the battle.
The ambush uses feigned retreat, an undefended city, fire, encirclement, and denial of escape. The text reports no survivor and twelve thousand dead, including women.
The king is hanged and then removed at sunset, continuing Deuteronomy's same-day burial limit even for an enemy ruler.
Ai's archaeological identification and occupation history are disputed; the narrative must not be presented as uncomplicated archaeological fact.
The Ebal altar and Torah inscription link conquest to Deuteronomy 27, but textual traditions differ regarding altar location, especially the Samaritan Pentateuch's Gerizim reading.

The closing public reading includes leaders, native-born residents, immigrants, women, and children. This inclusive access to Torah is a constructive thread that does not erase the preceding massacre.
safety_note: Never use Joshua 8 to justify ambush warfare against civilians, genocide, city burning, looting, execution, territorial conquest, or archaeological overclaiming. Keep Ai's dead visible while preserving the chapter's better closing movement: public Torah, shared assembly, peace offerings, and equal hearing for women, children, immigrants, leaders, and ordinary residents.
